papers.title: The disc origin of the Milky Way bulge: On the high velocity dispersion of metal-rich stars at low latitude
papers.author_names: Tristan Boin Paola Di Matteo Sergey Khoperskov Francesca Fragkoudi Soumavo Ghosh Françoise Combes Misha Haywood David Katz

abstract.text: Previous studies of the chemo-kinematic properties of stars in the Galactic bulge have revealed a puzzling trend. Along the bulge minor axis, and close to the Galactic plane, metal-rich stars display a higher line-of-sight velocity dispersion compared to metal-poor stars, while at higher latitudes metal-rich stars have lower velocity dispersions than metal-poor stars, similar to what is found in the Galactic disc. In this work, we re-examine this issue, by studying the dependence of line-of-sight velocity dispersions on metallicity and latitude in the latest APOGEE Data Release 17, confirming the results of previous works. We then analyse an N-body simulation of a Milky Way-like galaxy, also taking into account observational biases introduced by the APOGEE selection function. We show that the inversion in the line-of-sight velocity dispersion-latitude relation observed in the Galactic bulge can be reproduced by our model. We show that this inversion is a natural consequence of a scenario in which the bulge is a boxy/peanut-shaped structure, whose metal-rich and metal-poor stars mainly originate from the thin and thick disc of the Milky Way, respectively. Due to their cold kinematics, metal-rich, thin disc stars, are efficiently trapped in the boxy/peanut bulge, and, at low latitudes, show a strong barred morphology, which results in high velocity dispersions which are larger than those attained by the metal-poor populations. Extremely metal-rich stars in the Galactic bulge, which have received renewed attention in the literature, do follow the same trends as those of the metal-rich populations. The line-of-sight velocity-latitude relation observed in the Galactic bulge for metal-poor and metal-rich stars are thus both an effect of the intrinsic nature of the Galactic bulge and of the angle at which we observe it from the Sun.

brief.tldr: [research paper] The inversion of line-of-sight velocity dispersion with metallicity in the Galactic bulge—where metal-rich stars exhibit higher velocity dispersions than metal-poor stars at low latitudes—is explained by a secular evolution scenario in which the bulge forms from disc stars. Thin-disc, metal-rich stars are efficiently trapped in the boxy/peanut-shaped bulge due to vertical resonance trapping and bar-driven dynamics, leading to strong barred morphologies at low latitudes that amplify velocity dispersions. This effect, combined with the orientation of the bar relative to the Sun–Galactic center line, naturally reproduces the observed kinematic inversion, confirming that the bulge's structure and kinematics are primarily driven by disc-origin material rather than a distinct bulge formation mechanism.

source_fields.title: The disc origin of the Milky Way bulge: On the high velocity dispersion of metal-rich stars at low latitude

source_fields.sections_text: Results: The $N$-body simulation reproduces the observed velocity dispersion profiles of bulge stars, showing a reversal in the metallicity-velocity dispersion trend at $|b| < 5^\\circ$—where metal-poor stars are kinematically cooler than metal-rich ones—consistent with literature data, with metal-rich stars exhibiting a steeper $\\sigma_{vlos} - |b|$ slope of $-15.99 \\pm 1.39\\,\\text{km}\\,\\text{s}^{-1}\\,\\text{deg}^{-1}$ compared to $-8.07 \\pm 1.39\\,\\text{km}\\,\\text{s}^{-1}\\,\\text{deg}^{-1}$ for metal-poor stars. Footnotes: The section outlines methodological choices and references, including the use of a galactocentric frame, membership probability filtering, higher-resolution simulations, and noted uncertainties in velocity dispersion measurements. Appendix#2: A mock catalogue is constructed from an $N$-body simulation to match APOGEE observational dispersion profiles, accounting for distance uncertainties and selection biases, with particular attention to the well-sampled region near $\\ell = 0^\\circ$ and $b = 17^\\circ$. Appendix#3: The metal-rich population exhibits a stronger galactic bar signature than the metal-poor population, as quantified by the ratio of the $A_2/A_0$ Fourier coefficients across different radii. Appendix#4: This figure shows velocity dispersion maps for different stellar populations along bar-oriented directions, with isodensity contours indicating spatial distribution and structure. Conclusions: This study demonstrates that the observed velocity dispersion trends in the Galactic bulge, particularly the inversion between metal-rich and metal-poor stars, arise naturally from the differential kinematic response of cold, thin-disc metal-rich stars and hot, thick-disc metal-poor stars to bar instabilities, supporting a disc-origin scenario for the bulge where most of its mass formed from disc material. Introduction: This paper presents a novel analysis of APOGEE DR17 data to investigate the unexpected inverse velocity dispersion trend in the Galactic bulge, challenging existing disc-to-b/p bulge transformation models and providing critical observational constraints to test whether the bulge's kinematics can be fully explained by disc material reshaped through bar-driven instabilities. Observational data and model: The paper proposes a method that selects a sample of bulge field stars from the APOGEE DR17 catalogue using spatial, distance, and spectral criteria, and compares their properties to a dissipationless N-body simulation of the Milky Way's stellar components to study the bulge's structure and stellar populations.

catalog_assessment.evidence: Paper analyzes velocity dispersion trends in APOGEE DR17 data but focuses on statistical population properties, not object-level catalog of high-velocity stars. Uses simulation to explain observed trends.

papers.title: A Detailed Chemical Study of the Extreme Velocity Stars in the Galaxy
papers.author_names: Tyler Nelson Keith Hawkins Henrique Reggiani Diego Garza Rosemary F.G. Wyse Turner Woody

abstract.text: Two decades on, the study of hypervelocity stars is still in its infancy. These stars can provide novel constraints on the total mass of the Galaxy and its Dark Matter distribution. However how these stars are accelerated to such high velocities is unclear. Various proposed production mechanisms for these stars can be distinguished using chemo-dynamic tagging. The advent of Gaia and other large surveys have provided hundreds of candidate hyper velocity objects to target for ground based high resolution follow-up observations. We conduct high resolution spectroscopic follow-up observations of 16 candidate late-type hyper velocity stars using the Apache Point Observatory and the McDonald Observatory. We derive atmospheric parameters and chemical abundances for these stars. We measure up to 22 elements, including the following nucleosynthetic families: {\\\\alpha} (Mg, Si, Ca, Ti), light/Odd-Z (Na, Al, V, Cu, Sc), Fe-peak (Fe, Cr, Mn, Co, Ni, Zn), and Neutron Capture (Sr, Y, Zr, Ba, La, Nd, Eu). Our kinematic analysis shows one candidate is unbound, two are marginally bound, and the remainder are bound to the Galaxy. Finally, for the three unbound or marginally bound stars, we perform orbit integration to locate possible globular cluster or dwarf galaxy progenitors. We do not find any likely candidate systems for these stars and conclude that the unbound stars are likely from the the stellar halo, in agreement with the chemical results. The remaining bound stars are all chemically consistent with the stellar halo as well.

brief.tldr: [research paper] A high-resolution spectroscopic study of 16 late-type hyper-velocity stars reveals chemical abundances across 22 elements, enabling chemodynamic tagging to distinguish formation mechanisms. Kinematic analysis identifies one unbound and two marginally bound stars; orbit integrations fail to link any to known globular clusters or dwarf galaxies, supporting a stellar halo origin for unbound stars. All bound stars are chemically consistent with the stellar halo, suggesting that current HVS formation models—particularly Hills' mechanism and tidal stripping—are insufficient without significant dynamical heating or alternative pathways.

source_fields.title: A Detailed Chemical Study of the Extreme Velocity Stars in the Galaxy

source_fields.venue: Monthly notices of the Royal Astronomical Society
source_fields.journal_name: Monthly Notices of the Royal Astronomical Society
source_fields.sections_text: Results: Only one of the candidate HVS stars is confidently unbound to the Galaxy, with stars 5 and 8 marginally unbound, and kinematic analyses show that stars 7 and 8 likely originated from NGC 6205 and NGC 6341, while star 5 originated from Draco II, with no significant changes in results using a modified dark matter halo or LMC potential. Summary: This study confirms the HVS status of 16 candidate stars, finds they are largely bound with only one strongly unbound and no clear progenitor or accreted origin, and reports chemical abundances consistent with inner halo stars, challenging the prevailing view that the halo is predominantly accreted. Footnotes: The footnotes clarify definitions, observational methods, error handling, and limitations in stellar abundance measurements and Li detection. Abundances: Abundances for 22 elements are measured using the LoneStar abund module through $\\chi^2$ minimization of synthetic spectra, with quality control based on statistical thresholds, line sensitivity, and visual inspection, while accounting for metallicity-dependent line visibility and potential NLTE effects. Discussion: The only unbound star in the sample is consistent with a possible origin in the globular cluster NGC 5897, while no evidence supports LMC or low-α halo origins, and the star's chemical abundances are within measured uncertainties of NGC 5897's known composition, though further high-SNR observations are needed for confirmation. Introduction: This paper introduces a novel framework that reconciles the observed distribution and kinematics of hyper-velocity stars by extending beyond the Hills' mechanism to incorporate dynamical interactions in stellar clusters and accreted systems, thereby solving the long-standing problem of unexplained HVS origins and isotropy violations in Galactic velocity distributions. Data Properties: This section outlines the selection of halo velocity stars (HVSs) using diverse astrophysical methods and data sources, followed by high-resolution spectroscopic follow-up observations to study their properties and validate findings across different methodologies. Dynamical Analysis: This section uses a dynamic analysis with Gaia and Bailer-Jones distance data, propagated through Monte Carlo sampling and the MWPotential2014 Galactic model, to determine whether HVS candidates are unbound or marginally bound and to identify potential progenitor systems. Atmospheric Parameters: This section describes a combined spectroscopic and photometric workflow to accurately determine atmospheric parameters of metal-poor HVS candidates, using photometry to constrain effective temperature and surface gravity and spectroscopy to derive metallicity and microturbulence, while accounting for limitations in low-SNR data and non-local thermodynamic equilibrium effects. A Detailed Chemical Study of the Extreme Velocity Stars in the Galaxy: This section presents a detailed chemical analysis of extreme velocity stars in the galaxy to understand their origins, evolutionary histories, and potential connections to galactic dynamics and stellar populations.

abstract: Two decades on, the study of hypervelocity stars is still in its infancy. These stars can provide novel constraints on the total mass of the Galaxy and its Dark Matter distribution.
However how these stars are accelerated to such high velocities is unclear. Various proposed production mechanisms for these stars can be distinguished using chemo-dynamic tagging.
We derive atmospheric parameters and chemical abundances for these stars.
We do not find any likely candidate systems for these stars and conclude that the unbound stars are likely from the the stellar halo, in agreement with the chemical results.
The remaining bound stars are all chemically consistent with the stellar halo as well.
title: A Detailed Chemical Study of the Extreme Velocity Stars in the Galaxy

catalog_assessment.evidence: Presents high-resolution spectroscopic follow-up of 16 candidate hypervelocity stars with derived atmospheric parameters, chemical abundances, and kinematic analysis including orbit integration.

papers.title: Runaway BN supergiant star HD 93840: Progenitor of an imminent core-collapse supernova above the Galactic plane
papers.author_names: D. Weßmayer M. A. Urbaneja K. Butler N. Przybilla

abstract.text: We present a quantitative spectral analysis of the extreme nitrogen-enhanced supergiant HD 93840 (BN1 Ib) at an intermediate galactic latitude. Based on an optical high-resolution spectrum and complementary ultraviolet and infrared (spectro-)photometry, in addition to Gaia data, we carried out a full characterisation of the star's properties. We used both hydrostatic and unified (photosphere+wind) model atmospheres that account for deviations from local thermodynamic equilibrium. A highly unusual surface CNO-mixing signature and a marked stellar overluminosity compared to the mass imply a binary channel for the star's past evolution. The kinematics shows that it has reached its current position above the Galactic plane as a runaway star, likely ejected by the supernova explosion of its former companion star. Its current bulk composition, with a notably increased mean molecular weight due to core He- and progressed shell H-burning, suggests an advanced evolutionary stage. It is poised to yield a rare core-collapse supernova of a blue supergiant about ten OB star population scale heights above the Galactic disk relatively soon, contributing to the metal enrichment of the circumgalactic medium.

triage.label: rule-weak-llm-confirmed
triage.reason: Weak title match: runaway-related mechanisms LLM confirmed: Explicitly studies a runaway BN supergiant star, analyzes its kinematics as a runaway ejected by a supernova, and focuses on high-velocity/runaway star properties and origins.

brief.skipped_reason: weak rule match; retained with search metadata only

source_fields.sections_text: Results: The ADS solution provides the most accurate atmospheric parameters and elemental abundances for HD 93840, with excellent agreement between synthetic and observed spectra and a reliable fit to the SED, while showing consistently lower microturbulent velocities and a better reproduction of CNO abundances compared to FASTWIND and TLUSTY, particularly affecting metal abundance determinations at higher $\\xi$ values. Footnotes: The footnotes provide methodological details, observational justifications, and astrophysical context regarding stellar mass loss, spectral data quality, scale heights of stellar populations, and binary evolution timelines. Appendix#2: The distance to HD 93840 is constrained by spectroscopic and Gaia parallax methods, with ongoing debates over systematic biases in the parallax zero point and uncertainty estimates, leading to refined distance values that remain consistent within uncertainties. Appendix#3: The kinematic and orbital analysis of HD 93840 reveals a runaway supergiant with a highly elevated Galactic trajectory, indicating a disrupted binary origin and significant CNO mixing, placing it far from the typical location of massive stars in the Galaxy. Discussion: HD 93840 is a blue supergiant with extreme helium enrichment and high surface mixing, indicating a binary formation history and a massive helium-burning core, consistent with a runaway star originating from the Carina-Sagittarius spiral arm and evolving through a core-collapse supernova pathway. Methodology: The paper proposes a hybrid non-LTE approach using ATLAS12 combined with DETAIL and SURFACE for photospheric modeling, and a complementary unified wind+photosphere non-LTE model using FASTWIND, both to independently constrain the atmospheric parameters of HD 93840 by accounting for non-LTE effects and stellar wind dynamics. Introduction: This paper presents a comprehensive atmospheric and chemical analysis revealing that HD 93840 is a highly-evolved, overluminous blue supergiant runaway star with a rare progenitor pathway that will likely explode as a blue supergiant core-collapse supernova—offering a novel solution to the long-standing problem of metal enrichment in the galactic halo and circumgalactic medium via non-standard, high-altitude supernova events. Observational data: The study combines high-resolution optical spectra from FEROS with complementary UV, optical, and infrared photometric and spectroscopic data to enable a comprehensive analysis of HD 93840.

abstract: The kinematics shows that it has reached its current position above the Galactic plane as a runaway star, likely ejected by the supernova explosion of its former companion star.
title: Runaway BN supergiant star HD 93840: Progenitor of an imminent core-collapse supernova above the Galactic

catalog_assessment.evidence: Detailed observational study of single runaway supergiant HD 93840 with spectral analysis, Gaia data, kinematics, and composition measurements.
